title: Panic Disorder and Phobias
subtitle: The nurse helps a patient with panic attacks and phobias. Stay with the patient, use a calm voice and slow breathing; phobias improve with gradual exposure.

patient.name: Panic / Phobias
patient.admitDate: The nurse supports a patient with panic disorder or a phobia.

nursesNote: A panic attack is a sudden surge of intense fear with physical symptoms (palpitations, chest tightness, shortness of breath) that can mimic a heart attack and usually peaks within about 10 minutes. The nurse stays with the patient, uses a calm low voice with short directions, reduces stimulation, guides slow breathing, and reassures that they are safe. A phobia is an intense, persistent fear of a specific object or situation that leads to avoidance; it is treated with gradual exposure (desensitization), sometimes with SSRIs. The nurse teaches coping and relaxation skills once the patient is calmer.
providerOrders: Stay with the patient during a panic attack
Use a calm voice and reduce stimulation
Guide slow, deep breathing and reassure safety
Teach coping and relaxation skills once calmer

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about panic and phobias?

opts.text: A panic attack is a sudden surge of intense fear
Symptoms can mimic a heart attack
An attack usually peaks in about 10 minutes
The nurse should leave the patient alone
A phobia leads to avoidance
Phobias improve with gradual exposure
A calm, low voice helps
Slow, deep breathing lowers arousal
Detailed teaching works best mid-attack
Reducing stimulation helps
Reassure the patient they are safe
Exposure therapy is first-line for phobias

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order helping a patient in a panic attack

opts.text: Stay with the patient and ensure safety
Reduce stimulation; calm voice
Guide slow breathing
Reassure that it will pass
Teach coping skills once calmer
Sudden palpitations and a sense of doom
Avoiding elevators due to intense fear
Chest tightness peaking then easing
Refusing to leave home for fear of crowds
Shortness of breath feeling like dying
Strong fear of needles, treated with exposure

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A patient is hyperventilating, clutching the chest, terrified

opts.text: Stay and keep the patient safe
Speak calmly and simply
Coach slow breathing together
Give a PRN anxiolytic if ordered
Reassess and document
Staying with the patient during the attack
Saying 'just calm down, nothing is wrong'
Coaching slow, deep breathing
Forcing a phobic patient to face the fear at once
Using a calm voice and short directions
Leaving the panicking patient alone
Teaching relaxation skills once calmer
Giving detailed instructions mid-attack
